name: iron stove
shortDescription: A squat iron stove built for steady heat, heavy pots, and rooms that need warmth more than elegance. Its blackened plates remember years of soup, ash, boiled dye, and the small comfort of fire kept under control.
flavorText: “A hearth is a promise; an iron stove is the promise with hinges.”

name: mortar and pestle
shortDescription: A worn mortar and pestle for crushing seeds, salts, roots, powders, and the confidence of careless apprentices. The bowl is scarred by honest work, which makes it more trustworthy than any polished display piece.
flavorText: “Most remedies begin as something stubborn being persuaded into dust.”

name: refuser
shortDescription: A refuser is a peculiar alchemical tool made to reject, separate, and push unwanted essences out of a mixture. Good ones are prized by careful brewers, because a potion often becomes safe only after something has been firmly told to leave.
flavorText: “Refinement is not adding the right thing; sometimes it is refusing the wrong one.”

name: retort
shortDescription: A curved glass retort used to catch vapors, bend them through heat, and return them as something more valuable or more alarming. Its delicate neck makes alchemy look graceful right up to the moment a careless flame proves otherwise.
flavorText: “A retort lets a liquid become a rumor, then bottles the rumor again.”

name: acidic blob
shortDescription: An acidic blob quivers with a sour shine, as if a puddle learned appetite and poor manners. Handlers value it for dissolving, etching, and frightening thieves away from shelves that should never be touched barehanded.
flavorText: “Never store an acidic blob above anything you still like.”

name: scarce azure jelly
shortDescription: Scarce azure jelly glows a watery blue and moves with the lazy confidence of something that knows jars are temporary. Alchemists prize its cool residue, though collectors mostly want it because it looks like moonlight forgot to harden.
flavorText: “Beautiful slime sells best when the lid is tight.”

name: brown ooze
shortDescription: Brown ooze is an ugly, practical creature of rot, mud, and patient digestion. It is not glamorous stock, but tanners, waste-workers, and unpleasant experimenters know that useful things rarely arrive smelling heroic.
flavorText: “The brown ooze has no pride, which is why it survives nearly everything.”

name: dangerous death ooze
shortDescription: Dangerous death ooze is handled only by fools, experts, and people whose debts have become more frightening than monsters. It slides like spilled midnight and leaves behind the kind of silence even veteran hunters dislike.
flavorText: “If the jar is sweating black, do not ask for a discount.”

name: fire jelly
shortDescription: Fire jelly pulses with banked heat, glowing as if embers were trapped inside something that should have stayed wet. It is used in dangerous lamps, warming charms, and kitchen stunts that usually end with shouting.
flavorText: “Fire jelly is proof that flame resents being poured.”

name: giant amoeba
shortDescription: A giant amoeba is less an animal than a wandering appetite with transparent edges. Scholars call it fascinating; caravan guards call it something shorter while checking whether their boots are still attached.
flavorText: “The trouble with a giant amoeba is that it considers curiosity mutual.”

name: brain jelly
shortDescription: Brain jelly has a pale, folded look that makes even seasoned handlers speak more quietly around it. Whether it is truly clever or only shaped like a bad idea, buyers pay well for anything that might help thought become substance.
flavorText: “No one likes brain jelly until they need a question answered by something without a skull.”

name: jellyfish
shortDescription: A jellyfish is all drifting veil, faint sting, and seawater grace, too delicate for rough trade and too troublesome to ignore. Apothecaries want its sting, glassmakers admire its shape, and sailors prefer it at a distance.
flavorText: “The sea makes some things soft because it does not need them harmless.”

name: ooze
shortDescription: Plain ooze is the common sort: damp, slow, difficult to admire, and surprisingly useful to anyone studying decay or digestion. It has no charm at all, which makes its steady market even more insulting.
flavorText: “An ooze has no face, no manners, and no trouble finding work.”

name: pulsating lump
shortDescription: A pulsating lump is a sealed bundle of living uncertainty, moving just enough to keep everyone honest about how brave they feel. Alchemists buy it for rare reactions; sensible merchants keep it far from the lunch baskets.
flavorText: “If a lump has a heartbeat, charge for the fear as well.”

name: royal jelly
shortDescription: Royal jelly is a rich, gleaming secretion prized by healers, breeders, perfumers, and anyone selling the idea of vitality in a spoon. It spoils reputations faster than purses, because every buyer swears their source is the pure one.
flavorText: “Royal jelly proves that luxury sometimes begins in a hive.”

name: alligator snapping turtle
shortDescription: An alligator snapping turtle looks like a mossy stone that has grown jaws and a grudge. It is heavy, patient, and valued by traders who understand that some cargo can defend itself a little too well.
flavorText: “Do not mistake stillness for agreement.”

name: alligator snapping turtle shell
shortDescription: The shell of an alligator snapping turtle is ridged, dark, and tough enough to make shield-makers pause appreciatively. Its weight tells the whole story: slow life, hard water, and a creature that trusted armor more than speed.
flavorText: “A good shell is a lifetime of saying no.”

name: frog
shortDescription: A frog is humble marsh stock: quick-legged, wide-eyed, damp, and useful in ways city buyers often underestimate. Children call it charming, cooks call it possible dinner, and alchemists call it a reagent before anyone can stop them.
flavorText: “Every pond has a prince, a pest, and a profitable frog.”

name: snapping turtle
shortDescription: A snapping turtle is a compact lesson in patience and consequences, all shell, suspicion, and sudden bite. It travels poorly but sells reliably to cooks, shell-workers, and collectors who believe danger looks better in a crate.
flavorText: “The snapping turtle only needs one quick moment in a slow life.”

name: snapping turtle shell
shortDescription: A snapping turtle shell is smaller than its giant cousin’s but still dense, durable, and full of workshop promise. Carvers, charmers, and armor-patchers value the honest curve of it, especially when cleaned by someone else.
flavorText: “Small armor is still armor if it saved its owner long enough.”

name: scarce spiny frog
shortDescription: A scarce spiny frog is a startling little creature, bristling with enough points to make curious hands reconsider. Its rarity gives it charm, but its temper ensures most admiration happens through glass.
flavorText: “Some frogs ask to be kissed. This one asks to be left alone.”

name: toad
shortDescription: A toad is warty, calm, and unfairly blamed for curses it probably did not write. Hedge-healers, swamp witches, and practical children all know it has more uses than beauty ever needed.
flavorText: “A toad never argues with insults; it simply waits for rain.”

name: fabled emperor scorpion
shortDescription: The fabled emperor scorpion is a dark, commanding creature with claws like polished threats and a tail that makes conversation shorter. It is bought by collectors, venom-workers, and nobles who mistake dangerous pets for personality.
flavorText: “An emperor scorpion does not rule a kingdom; it rules the hand that reaches too close.”

name: scarce golden scorpion
shortDescription: A scarce golden scorpion glitters like a lost coin until it moves, at which point everyone remembers why glass jars were invented. Its beauty attracts collectors, but its venom keeps negotiations brief and respectful.
flavorText: “Beautiful enough for a cabinet. Venomous enough for a will.”

name: scarce jumping spider
shortDescription: A scarce jumping spider is small, bright-eyed, and far too aware of distances for anyone’s comfort. It is prized by charmers and poisoners, though shopkeepers mostly remember the time one vanished into the account books.
flavorText: “The price drops if the buyer asks where it went.”

name: scarce orb spider
shortDescription: A scarce orb spider spins webbing so fine it seems drawn from moonlit thread. Silk-workers admire the pattern, alchemists want the glands, and superstitious buyers leave offerings beside the cage.
flavorText: “An orb spider writes geometry in hunger.”

name: notable redback spider
shortDescription: A notable redback spider carries its warning like a painted seal, small but impossible to ignore once noticed. Venom buyers respect it, collectors display it, and foolish handlers learn how quickly a tiny creature can become the whole room’s concern.
flavorText: “Red on the back means the spider has already signed the warning.”

name: scorpion
shortDescription: A common scorpion is a hard little survivor, all pincers, sand-colored patience, and business at the end of its tail. It may not be rare, but rarity matters less when the sting is personal.
flavorText: “A scorpion is never large until it is in your boot.”

name: market spider
shortDescription: A market spider is the ordinary trade kind, sold in jars for pest control, venom study, or unsettling gifts. It earns its keep quietly, provided the lid stays honest and no one names it after a customer.
flavorText: “Every market has spiders; this one comes with a price tag.”

name: tarantella
shortDescription: A tarantella is a large, dramatic spider famous for its restless legs and the uneasy stories told around its bite. Musicians make jokes about it, healers do not, and dancers prefer not to explain the connection twice.
flavorText: “Some creatures inspire songs because screaming lacks rhythm.”

name: notable trapdoor spider
shortDescription: A notable trapdoor spider is a patient ambusher, famous for turning empty ground into a sudden problem. Its burrow-craft fascinates hunters and engineers, though both groups prefer examples that are not currently occupied.
flavorText: “The trapdoor spider teaches that a door may be a mouth in disguise.”

name: wolf spider
shortDescription: A wolf spider hunts without webs, quick on the floor and honest about its intentions. It is valued by pest-keepers and disliked by sleepers, especially those who learn too late that the ceiling is not the only place to look.
flavorText: “The wolf spider does not wait for prey to blunder; it goes looking.”

name: scarce boring beetle
shortDescription: A scarce boring beetle can worry through wood, cork, and patience with the steady confidence of a tiny carpenter gone bad. Millers and shipwrights pay to identify it quickly; smugglers sometimes pay for less respectable reasons.
flavorText: “A boring beetle is dull only to people who do not own beams.”

name: notable boulder beetle
shortDescription: A notable boulder beetle is round, armored, and stubborn enough to look carved rather than hatched. Children admire it, collectors weigh it, and anyone moving crates learns that even insects can have opinions about momentum.
flavorText: “The boulder beetle does not hurry; it arrives with impact.”

name: bumblebee
shortDescription: A bumblebee is a fuzzy little worker with more importance than its size suggests. Orchards, herb gardens, and honey sellers all depend on its busy wandering, though it accepts no contracts and observes no office hours.
flavorText: “A bee does not read the harvest ledger, but it writes half of it.”

name: blue butterfly
shortDescription: A blue butterfly catches light like a scrap of clear sky that learned to flutter. It sells well to collectors, charm-makers, and anyone who believes luck looks better with wings.
flavorText: “Blue butterflies are small enough to vanish and bright enough to be missed.”

name: crimson butterfly
shortDescription: A crimson butterfly flashes through the air like a petal cut from sunset. Court perfumers and festival decorators prize its color, while superstitious travelers take its arrival as either blessing or warning depending on the road.
flavorText: “Crimson wings make even a quiet garden look accused of romance.”

name: green butterfly
shortDescription: A green butterfly hides beautifully among leaves until it moves, then betrays itself as something more delicate than foliage. Herb sellers like to sketch it on labels, claiming customers trust remedies touched by living green.
flavorText: “The green butterfly is what a leaf dreams of becoming when no one is looking.”

name: lime butterfly
shortDescription: A lime butterfly is bright, sharp-colored, and almost cheerful enough to seem artificial. It is popular with children, spring markets, and dyers searching for a color that refuses to behave quietly.
flavorText: “Some wings whisper. Lime wings giggle.”

name: pink butterfly
shortDescription: A pink butterfly has the soft charm of orchard blossoms and folded love letters. It is fragile trade stock, but buyers forgive the trouble because tenderness is easier to sell when it flutters.
flavorText: “Pink butterflies make even hard bargainers lower their voices.”

name: purple butterfly
shortDescription: A purple butterfly carries a royal little shade without the burden of any actual crown. Collectors favor it for display cases, and fortune tellers insist it appears near choices people will later pretend were obvious.
flavorText: “Purple wings lend dignity to even the shortest flight.”

name: red butterfly
shortDescription: A red butterfly is vivid, restless, and difficult to ignore against pale walls or winter branches. Some villages call it a sign of passion, others of fever, which has never hurt its price among charm-sellers.
flavorText: “Red wings are never merely passing through; they announce themselves.”

name: royal blue butterfly
shortDescription: A royal blue butterfly has a deeper, richer color than common blues, like enamel warmed by sunlight. It is a favorite of nobles who want nature displayed in colors that match their tiles.
flavorText: “Royal blue is what happens when the sky learns manners.”

name: royal purple butterfly
shortDescription: A royal purple butterfly is a tiny aristocrat of the garden, all velvet color and fragile arrogance. It fetches absurd prices from collectors who insist the shade is rarer than pride.
flavorText: “A royal purple butterfly needs no throne; it has already convinced the room.”

name: teal butterfly
shortDescription: A teal butterfly shifts between green and blue with every angle, making it look half pond-light and half sky. Jewelers love its color because customers do, though none of them can agree what to call it.
flavorText: “Teal is the color of water refusing to answer simply.”

name: yellow butterfly
shortDescription: A yellow butterfly is a small moving coin of sunlight, common enough to delight and fragile enough to make capture feel rude. Farmers watch for it near early flowers; children watch because children are sensible.
flavorText: “Yellow wings make a field look richer without changing the harvest.”

name: eastern moth
shortDescription: An eastern moth is a dusky, soft-winged night visitor from lamp roads and warm window ledges. Its wings look modest until opened fully, where the patterns seem borrowed from old curtains and secret maps.
flavorText: “Moths do not chase flame out of foolishness; they chase answers no one else can see.”

name: giant ant
shortDescription: A giant ant is disciplined, tireless, and strong enough to make farmers wonder whether admiration is allowed. Traders want its chitin, alchemists want its acid, and everyone respects how little it cares for being alone.
flavorText: “One giant ant is cargo. A line of them is a border dispute.”

name: giant beetle
shortDescription: A giant beetle wears its shell like a traveling fortress, glossy, hard, and heavier than it has any right to be. Armorers study the plates, children climb the empty ones, and wagon drivers curse the living sort in several dialects.
flavorText: “The beetle proves armor can be born before it is forged.”

name: giant blowfly
shortDescription: A giant blowfly is all glassy eyes, dirty wings, and the unpleasant talent of finding ruin before people admit it exists. It is useful to corpse-watchers and poisoners, but no market stall becomes more popular by displaying one openly.
flavorText: “Where a giant blowfly lands, the truth is already starting to smell.”
